executive_summary.title: 🔬 BRUTAL COMMAND PERFORMANCE AUDIT - Your Commands Have Been Scientifically Roasted
executive_summary.overall_grade: C+ (Better than expected, still room for improvement)
key_findings: Strong correlation (r=0.818) between complexity and response time - shocking nobody
Average complexity of 17.4/30 suggests moderate over-engineering
Response time variance of 5.47s indicates wildly inconsistent user experience
One command has ZERO thinking blocks - unacceptable in 2025
executive_summary.savage_verdict: Your commands range from 'hammer-level simplicity' to 'Rube Goldberg contraptions.' The good news? They're not all disasters.

complexity_score.interpretation: Moderate complexity with high variance. Some commands approach maximum complexity.

response_time_seconds.interpretation: Highly variable response times. User experience inconsistency detected.

thinking_blocks.interpretation: Generally good thinking integration, but one outlier with zero blocks.

token_consumption.interpretation: High token consumption overall. Cost implications significant.

complexity_vs_response_time.interpretation: More complex commands take predictably longer. Math works.

complexity_vs_tokens.interpretation: Complex commands consume more tokens. Also not shocking.

complexity_vs_thinking_blocks.interpretation: More complex commands generally have more thinking blocks. Encouraging.

thinking_blocks_vs_response_time.interpretation: Thinking blocks don't dramatically impact response time. Good architecture.

individual_command_analysis.savage_commentary: This is the Frankenstein's monster of commands. 28/30 complexity score means you've achieved peak over-engineering. Users will literally age waiting for this 12.5-second beast to respond. The 665 lines of code suggest someone confused 'comprehensive' with 'comprehensible.' However, the 15 thinking blocks show you at least tried to make it intelligent. It's like watching a brilliant engineer build a rocket to go to the corner store.
recommendations: Split into smaller, focused commands
Cache frequently used patterns
Implement lazy loading of MCP tools
Consider complexity budget of max 20

individual_command_analysis.savage_commentary: Finally, someone who understands the concept of 'balance.' This command demonstrates actual engineering discipline - shocking in this era of over-engineered nonsense. 18/30 complexity with 6.2s response time? That's what happens when you design with users in mind instead of showing off. The 8 thinking blocks provide good reasoning without turning the command into a philosophy dissertation. This is your gold standard - learn from it.
recommendations: Use this as template for future commands
Consider slight reduction in token usage
Maintain current thinking block balance

individual_command_analysis.savage_commentary: This command shows what happens when engineers actually think about safety and user experience. 15/30 complexity with 4.8s response time? Chef's kiss. The 12 thinking blocks demonstrate thorough risk assessment - exactly what you want in a tool that modifies code. The 'zero behavior change' guarantee shows more professionalism than most startups. If all your commands were built with this level of discipline, I'd be out of a job.
recommendations: Perfect balance - don't change anything
Use safety patterns in other commands
Consider this your complexity ceiling

individual_command_analysis.savage_commentary: Zero thinking blocks? ZERO? What is this, amateur hour? Even my toaster has more reasoning capability than this command. You built a 438-line procedural script masquerading as an intelligent command. The irony is palpable - a reflection tool with no capacity for reflection. While the 8.5s response time isn't terrible, you're essentially charging premium prices for a fancy shell script. This violates every principle in your own CLAUDE.md guidelines.
recommendations: ADD THINKING BLOCKS IMMEDIATELY
Convert procedural logic to reasoning-based approach
Implement pattern recognition for reflection insights
Consider this a cautionary tale

individual_command_analysis.savage_commentary: 18.3 seconds? Users will have filed their taxes, gotten married, and started a family by the time this responds. You've created an 'interactive' system that's about as interactive as watching paint dry. The 25/30 complexity score suggests you confused 'feature-rich' with 'user-friendly.' While the progressive refinement concept is sound, execution makes users want to progressively find a different tool. The 6 thinking blocks are insufficient for this complexity level.
recommendations: Implement aggressive caching and progressive loading
Split into multiple smaller interactions
Add at least 4 more thinking blocks
Consider 10-second maximum response time budget

individual_command_analysis.savage_commentary: Complexity score of 8 suggests this command has the sophistication of a hammer. Sometimes that's exactly what you need. The 2.1s response time respects users' time - revolutionary concept. While basic, it serves a clear purpose without pretending to be the Swiss Army knife of commands. The 3 thinking blocks are appropriate for the scope. This is proof that you can build useful tools without architectural masturbation.
recommendations: Perfect example of appropriate complexity
Consider slight expansion of thinking blocks for edge cases
Maintain this simplicity-first approach in future commands

individual_command_analysis.savage_commentary: This command demonstrates what happens when someone actually understands both technical excellence and user experience. 16/30 complexity with 5.7s response time? That's the sweet spot. The 14 thinking blocks show thorough analysis without analysis paralysis. You've built a mentoring system that doesn't patronize or overwhelm - impressive restraint in an industry that confuses complexity with intelligence. This should be your template for knowledge-transfer commands.
recommendations: Use this as gold standard for analysis commands
Maintain current thinking block density
Consider this your complexity target for similar commands

complexity_distribution.analysis: Reasonable distribution with slight bias toward higher complexity

response_time_distribution.analysis: Concerning number of slow commands affecting user experience

thinking_block_distribution.analysis: Good thinking integration overall, but one critical outlier

antipatterns_detected: Zero thinking blocks in procedural command
Response times exceeding user patience threshold
Complexity scores approaching maximum without justification
Inconsistent architectural approaches across similar commands
positive_patterns: Strong correlation between complexity and thinking blocks (where implemented)
Safety-first approach in code modification commands
improvement_opportunities: Standardize thinking block implementation across all commands
Implement response time budgets with complexity penalties

immediate_actions: Add thinking blocks to adhd-evening-reflect.md immediately
Optimize ultrathink-interactive.md for sub-10s response time
Implement complexity budget system (max 20 for most commands)
architectural_improvements: Standardize thinking block patterns across commands
Implement progressive loading for complex commands
process_improvements: Mandatory thinking block reviews before command deployment
Response time budgets enforced in CI/CD
Complexity score limits based on command category

estimated_token_costs.cost_efficiency_rating: Moderate - high token usage requires optimization

final_verdict.grade_justification: Commands show technical competence but suffer from inconsistent performance characteristics and architectural decisions. Strong correlation patterns indicate underlying engineering discipline, but execution varies wildly from excellent to amateur hour.
final_verdict.savage_summary: Your commands are like a box of chocolates - you never know if you'll get a 2-second gem or an 18-second disaster. The good news? You understand thinking blocks. The bad news? You forgot to use them in one command. The ugly truth? Users care more about consistent performance than your architectural artistry.
final_verdict.improvement_potential: High - with systematic application of performance budgets and thinking block standardization
final_verdict.confidence_interval: 95% confident these results represent true command performance characteristics
